name: Computer & Technology Supplies
description: Counselor denied a laptop, printer, or software, claiming a local budget cap (e.g. $500) or that tech is not covered.

vrcArguments.label: VRC claims a flat $500 cap on computer purchases.
vrcArguments.correction: VA regulations and M28C guidelines contain no flat dollar cap for necessary supplies. Supplies should be authorized based on individual rehabilitation necessity.

vrcArguments.label: VRC claims a laptop is not required because the school has a public computer lab.
vrcArguments.correction: Under 38 C.F.R. § 21.220, if lack of a personal computer puts the veteran at a peer disadvantage or if disability limits computer lab use, a computer package should be authorized.

vrcArguments.label: VRC states books/supplies are covered by a fixed stipend like the GI Bill.
vrcArguments.correction: Chapter 31 is not governed by the Chapter 33 book-stipend cap, but supplies still require VA authorization under the applicable necessity standard.

evidenceChecklist.text: Official course syllabus showing computer or software requirements
School policy letter stating a personal computer is required for all enrolled students
Personal statement of necessity outlining why a personal computer is required due to disability or peer disadvantages
Medical letter or OT recommendation outlining physical/cognitive need for adaptive technology

citations.relevance: Authorizes provision of necessary books, supplies, and equipment.
Establishes policy that VA will furnish necessary supplies for training.
Governs computer packages based on peer disadvantage or disability.
Governs provision of services, supplies, and equipment, prohibiting flat spending caps.

rebuttalPushback: VA often pushes back claiming that they have a standard package. Rebut by requesting an individualized analysis tied to your training syllabus and disability restrictions under 38 C.F.R. § 21.212.

name: 48-Month Entitlement Extension
description: Counselor refused to extend training beyond 48 months or extend the 12-year period of eligibility.

vrcArguments.label: VRC claims the 48-month limit is statutory and cannot be extended.
vrcArguments.correction: 38 U.S.C. § 3105(c) and 38 C.F.R. § 21.78 authorize extensions beyond 48 months for veterans with a Serious Employment Handicap (SEH).

vrcArguments.label: VRC asserts your 12-year basic eligibility window is closed and cannot be bypassed.
vrcArguments.correction: 38 C.F.R. § 21.44 provides that the eligibility period should be extended if the veteran has an SEH and requires training to overcome it.

evidenceChecklist.text: Initial VRC evaluation document showing a Serious Employment Handicap (SEH) finding
Copy of your current signed IWRP/IPE detailing the course of study
Official transcripts showing incomplete coursework and credits remaining
Labor market reports showing that the current target goal is the only viable path to suitable employment

citations.relevance: Authorizes services beyond 48 months to overcome a Serious Employment Handicap.
Authorizes extension of the basic period of eligibility for veterans with an SEH.
Defines VREO/VRC authority to approve programs exceeding 48 months.
Outlines serious employment handicap (SEH) definitions and counselor decision boundaries.

rebuttalPushback: VA will assert that the 48-month cap is strict. Rebut by showing that you have an SEH, meaning your disabilities are severe enough to create a significant barrier to employment, which triggers the extension rules.

name: Retroactive Induction / GI Bill Restore
description: Counselor denied retroactive authorization for past training, claiming you cannot restore GI Bill months already used.

vrcArguments.label: VRC claims retroactive induction cannot cover periods prior to your formal application date.
vrcArguments.correction: 38 C.F.R. § 21.282 and manual M28C.V.B.6 allow retroactive induction covering past periods of training prior to application.

vrcArguments.label: VRC asserts retroactive adjustments are barred if Chapter 33 Post-9/11 GI Bill was already utilized.
vrcArguments.correction: VA guidelines establish that past GI Bill usage can be adjusted to Chapter 31, and utilized GI Bill months restored to the veteran.

evidenceChecklist.text: DD-214 and VA Rating Decisions proving you held a disability rating during the past training
Itemized tuition invoices and receipts proving you paid for the school terms
Official transcripts showing completed courses and passing grades during the retroactive terms
Approved IWRP detailing a vocational goal directly matching the past program of study

citations.relevance: Authorizes retroactive adjustments and reimbursements for past training.
Defines rules for retroactive induction effective dates.
Sets detailed operational procedures for VRCs to process retroactive adjustments and restore GI Bill months under sections 6.03 (self-pay) and 6.04 (Chapter 33).

rebuttalPushback: VA often claims that retroactive induction is only for extreme cases. Point to M28C.V.B.6 which outlines conditions for self-pay and Chapter 33 transfers.

name: Vocational Feasibility Rebuttal
description: Counselor declared you "not feasible" to achieve a vocational goal, attempting to close your case without services.

vrcArguments.label: VRC denied feasibility based on standard aptitude tests or a brief review rather than individualized evaluation.
vrcArguments.correction: Feasibility decisions require a comprehensive, multidisciplinary assessment under 38 C.F.R. § 21.50.

vrcArguments.label: VRC resolved clinical or vocational ambiguities against the veteran.
vrcArguments.correction: 38 C.F.R. § 21.57 directs VRCs to resolve reasonable doubt in favor of the veteran.

evidenceChecklist.text: Physician or therapist statement explaining how you can work with specific accommodations
Documentation of recent volunteer work, courses completed, or hobbies showing functional capacity
Copy of the VRC assessment report detailing specific objections

citations.relevance: Requires VA to conduct initial evaluations to determine vocational feasibility.
Commands VRCs to resolve doubts in favor of the veteran.
Provides for Extended Evaluation (up to 12 months) when feasibility is uncertain.
Governs VRC feasibility determinations and guidelines for graduate education and advanced degree approvals.

rebuttalPushback: Counselors sometimes use unfeasibility findings to exit veterans who require complex services. Rebut by requesting an Extended Evaluation under 38 C.F.R. § 21.74 to test your capacity with support.

name: Counselor Non-Response / Delay
description: Your VRC has stopped responding to emails/calls, missed appointments, or delayed authorizing classes/vouchers.

vrcArguments.label: Counselor does not respond or states that they have high caseloads and cannot follow up.
vrcArguments.correction: M28C service standards outline timely case management. Long communication gaps disrupt active training plans and run counter to the VA's duty to assist.

evidenceChecklist.text: Log of emails sent with read receipts or date stamps showing no reply
Interactive contact timeline entered below outlining method and result
Letter from school certifying official stating classes are at risk of drop due to lack of auth

citations.relevance: Establishes the VA Duty to Assist claimants throughout the benefits process.
Governs case manager responsibilities to coordinate, schedule, and maintain participant progress.
Sets casework procedures and timelines for counselor response and service provision.

rebuttalPushback: VRCs may claim they did not receive your request. Protect yourself by uploading documentation through QuickSubmit so the date is legally stamped in your VA file.

name: School Tuition / Books Unpaid
description: School term starts soon or has started, but VA has not authorized tuition payments or book vouchers.

vrcArguments.label: Counselor states the authorization will be processed when workload allows.
vrcArguments.correction: VA should coordinate tuition and supplies prior to term start to prevent veterans from facing drop deadlines or book shortages.

evidenceChecklist.text: Official term class schedule showing start date
Unpaid school account statement or invoice showing billing deadline
Email or statement from School Certifying Official indicating lack of active VA authorization

citations.relevance: Dictates rules for payment of school tuition and fees directly by VA.
Provides for required books, tools, and supplies prior to the commencement of courses.
Outlines standard business processes for generating authorizations in Tungsten portal.

rebuttalPushback: VA may point to school billing systems. Check with your School Certifying Official (SCO) to confirm if the VA has submitted authorizations in the portal.

name: Case Closed / Discontinued
description: Your case was closed or placed in interrupted status due to supposed lack of cooperation or progress.

vrcArguments.label: VRC closed the case claiming you failed to cooperate or did not attend appointments.
vrcArguments.correction: VA should issue a prior written warning outlining exact actions required to avoid closure, and offer a supervisor conference.

vrcArguments.label: VRC closed the case without issuing a formal written decision and statement of appeal rights.
vrcArguments.correction: 38 U.S.C. § 5104 requires written decision notices with detailed rationales and appeal options for benefits terminations.

evidenceChecklist.text: Lack of written 30-day intent to discontinue letter in your possession
Evidence of emails or letters sent to counselor during the closure period proving cooperation
Doctor note outlining medical emergency that prevented attendance at the missed appointment

citations.relevance: Requires VA to provide written notice of decisions affecting benefits, including appeal rights.
Governs procedures for interrupting a veteran's program of rehabilitation.
Details standard procedures for case discontinuation and the required warning notice.

rebuttalPushback: VRCs sometimes close cases to clear caseload metrics. Rebut by showing that you did not receive the warning letter or that you responded within the window.

name: Graduate School Denied
description: Counselor denied graduate school or advanced degree training, claiming Chapter 31 only covers entry-level bachelor degrees.

vrcArguments.label: VRC claims VR&E only rehabilitates to entry-level jobs and cannot pay for masters or professional degrees.
vrcArguments.correction: VA guidelines allow graduate training if it is required for entry into the target field or if severe disabilities restrict placement to fields requiring advanced degrees.

evidenceChecklist.text: O*NET or BLS printout showing target job requires a graduate degree for entry
3 local entry-level job listings in the field showing a Master's/Juris Doctor is mandatory
Medical statement showing physical limits block lower-level roles but allow advanced roles

citations.relevance: Dictates the selection and formulation of the vocational goal.
Requires that the rehabilitation plan enable the veteran to attain suitable employment.
Confirms advanced degrees may be approved if required to overcome employment barriers.

rebuttalPushback: VA will assert that entry-level employment is sufficient. Rebut by showing that your service-connected disabilities restrict entry to lower-level positions, making advanced education the viable path to a suitable career.
